judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is stronger: it correctly identifies the shared RESULTS_DB fixed-path collision risk and prescribes per-engineer overrides, while Report 1 dismisses that same conftest.py detail as likely-dead code and misses the collision entirely; both reports are otherwise comparable on registry.py grouping and the F-06 policy-decision flag.

report_1.evidence: Three independent one-line edits in an 11-line file — cheap enough to be one engineer's single small commit rather than three people touching the same tiny file.

report_2.evidence: I'd keep all three on one engineer rather than split by finding — the file is small enough that splitting three people onto it just creates avoidable diff/merge overhead for no parallelism benefit.

report_1.evidence: Wave 1 (parallel start): Groups 1-4 all start immediately afterward — none of them depend on each other... Group 3 — pipeline/registry.py... cheap enough to be one engineer's single small commit

report_2.evidence: Wave 1 (fully parallel, start immediately): Batches A, B, C, D, and the verify-only half of E (F-08). All five touch disjoint files, so they can run concurrently... I'd keep all three on one engineer rather than split by finding

report_1.evidence: F-06 — needs a number, not just direction, before it's assignable... This is a scoring-policy decision (it changes every future score's partial-credit boundary) rather than a mechanical bug fix; an engineer left to invent numbers under time pressure will produce an arbitrary, unreviewed policy change. Get explicit target thresholds from whoever owns rubric policy first.

report_2.evidence: F-06 — don't assign as "tighten the thresholds."... This changes the actual scores the pipeline assigns to real saved model outputs... Whoever owns rubric/grading policy needs to supply target threshold values before this becomes code.

tests/conftest.py:4 defines RESULTS_DB defaulting to a single fixed path, /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db, overridable only via an env var... any engineer whose fix adds a test that exercises scoring persistence (plausible for Batch D or E) needs to set their own RESULTS_DB — otherwise concurrent local test runs on the same machine could read/write the same file.
